facts.title: A mountainous country
facts.fact: About 70% of South Korea is mountainous, so cities cluster in valleys and along the coast. Hiking is one of the most popular weekend activities.
facts.korean_en: There are many mountains in Korea.
facts.note: 주말에 등산을 해요 = "I hike on the weekend." 등산 literally means "climb-mountain."

facts.title: Jeju, the volcanic island
facts.fact: Jeju Island off the south coast is volcanic and home to Hallasan, South Korea's highest peak at 1,947 m. It is a favorite honeymoon and holiday destination.
facts.korean_en: Jeju Island is a beautiful island.

facts.title: The Han River through Seoul
facts.fact: The Han River runs through the middle of Seoul. Its riverside parks are popular for picnics, bike rides, and ordering fried chicken by delivery.
facts.korean_en: I ride a bicycle at the Han River.
facts.note: **치맥** (치킨 + 맥주, "chicken + beer") by the river is a beloved tradition.

facts.title: Four distinct seasons
facts.fact: Korea has four clear seasons: spring with cherry blossoms, hot humid summer, crisp colorful autumn, and a cold dry winter with snow.
facts.note: **사계절** = "four seasons." 벚꽃 (cherry blossom) season in spring is a big event.

facts.title: Seoraksan, the autumn jewel
facts.fact: Seoraksan in the northeast is one of Korea's most loved national parks, famous for jagged granite peaks and brilliant autumn foliage. Crowds arrive each fall to see the red and gold leaves.
facts.korean_en: In autumn the foliage on Seoraksan is beautiful.
facts.note: **단풍** season is so popular that people say 단풍 구경 ("foliage viewing"), just like 벚꽃 구경 in spring.

facts.title: The west-coast tidal flats
facts.fact: The wide mudflats (갯벌) along the west coast are among the world's largest, and several were named a UNESCO World Heritage site in 2021. They teem with crabs, clams, and migratory birds.
facts.korean_en: There are wide tidal flats on the west coast.
facts.note: The west coast has a huge tidal range, so the **갯벌** appears and disappears with the tides every day.

facts.title: Dokdo, the easternmost islets
facts.fact: Dokdo is a pair of small rocky islets in the East Sea and the easternmost point of South Korea. Though tiny, they hold deep symbolic importance for Koreans.
facts.korean_en: Dokdo is at the eastern end of Korea.
facts.note: **동해** means "East Sea," the body of water where Dokdo sits.

facts.title: Ulleungdo, the remote volcanic isle
facts.fact: Ulleungdo is a steep volcanic island far out in the East Sea, reached by a long ferry ride. It is known for dramatic cliffs, squid fishing, and a slower way of life.
facts.korean_en: You go to Ulleungdo by boat.
facts.note: **배로 가다** = "to go by boat." The particle 로/으로 means "by (means of)."

facts.title: Jangma, the summer monsoon
facts.fact: Jangma is the rainy monsoon season that usually arrives in late June and lasts into July. It brings heavy rain, high humidity, and lots of umbrellas.
facts.korean_en: In summer the monsoon season begins.
facts.note: During **장마**, people often say 비가 많이 와요 ("it rains a lot"). 오다 ("to come") is the verb used for rain.

facts.title: The Nakdong, the southern lifeline
facts.fact: The Nakdong River is the longest river in South Korea, flowing south through the Yeongnam region to the sea near Busan. Its broad estuary is an important stopover for migratory birds.
facts.korean_en: The Nakdong River is the longest river in Korea.
facts.note: **가장 + adjective** makes the superlative: 가장 긴 = "the longest."

facts.title: The Geum River of the center-west
facts.fact: The Geum River winds through the central-western provinces, passing cities like Daejeon and Gongju before reaching the west coast. It watered the heartland of the ancient Baekje kingdom.
facts.korean_en: The Geum River passes through Chungcheong Province.

facts.title: Seoul's vast subway
facts.fact: The Seoul metropolitan subway is one of the largest and busiest systems in the world, with more than 20 lines reaching deep into the surrounding cities. It is clean, punctual, and easy to use with a transit card.
facts.korean_en: In Seoul I often take the subway.
facts.note: **지하철** literally means "underground iron (rail)." Each station is a 역.

facts.title: Seoul, the crowded capital
facts.fact: Seoul is the capital and largest city, home to roughly half the country's population when its greater metropolitan area is counted. The city is ringed by mountains and split by the Han River.
facts.korean_en: Seoul is the capital of Korea.
facts.note: **수도** = "capital." 서울은 인구가 많아요 = "Seoul has a large population."

facts.title: A peninsula on three seas
facts.fact: Korea is a peninsula bordered by sea on three sides: the East Sea, the West Sea (Yellow Sea), and the South Sea. This long coastline makes seafood a central part of the diet.
facts.korean_en: Korea is surrounded by sea on three sides.

facts.title: The many southern islands
facts.fact: The South Sea is dotted with thousands of small islands, many protected within Hallyeohaesang National Park. Their calm waters and green slopes make the area a scenic boating destination.
facts.korean_en: There are many small islands in the South Sea.
facts.note: **남해** = "South Sea" (남 "south" + 해 "sea"). Compare 동해 (East Sea) and 서해 (West Sea).

facts.title: Limestone caves of Gangwon
facts.fact: The mountainous Gangwon region holds famous limestone caves such as Hwanseongul, filled with stalactites and underground streams. Visitors walk lit paths through the cool, dripping chambers.
facts.korean_en: There are large caves in Gangwon Province.
facts.note: **동굴** = "cave." 강원도 is the rugged northeastern province best known for mountains and caves.

facts.title: Hot springs and bathhouses
facts.fact: Natural hot springs in towns like Onyang and Yuseong have drawn bathers for centuries. Soaking in warm mineral water is a popular way to relax, especially in cold weather.
facts.korean_en: In winter I go to the hot springs.

facts.title: The Taebaek Mountain Range
facts.fact: The Taebaek Mountains form the rugged spine of the peninsula, running down the eastern side and acting as a watershed for many rivers. The range is sometimes called the backbone of Korea.
facts.korean_en: The Taebaek Mountains are in the east.
